_meta.title: Network Layer Ipv4 Ipv6 Subnetting Cidr

questions.question: What is the broadcast address for the subnet 10.0.0.0/26?
questions.explanation: A /26 subnet has 64 addresses. The network address is 10.0.0.0 and the broadcast address is 10.0.0.63. Usable hosts are 10.0.0.1 through 10.0.0.62.

questions.question: Which field exists in the IPv4 header but is removed from the IPv6 header?
questions.explanation: IPv6 removes the header checksum because the transport layer (TCP/UDP) already provides error detection. This avoids recomputing the checksum at every router hop.

questions.question: How many usable host addresses are in a /30 subnet?
questions.explanation: A /30 subnet has 4 total addresses. Subtract the network address (all host bits 0) and the broadcast address (all host bits 1) to get 2 usable hosts. This is commonly used for point-to-point links.

questions.question: What does CIDR notation 192.168.0.0/22 specify?
options: A network that aggregates 4 Class C networks (192.168.0.0–192.168.3.255)
questions.explanation: A /22 prefix covers 1024 addresses (2^10), aggregating the four /24 networks 192.168.0.0, 192.168.1.0, 192.168.2.0, and 192.168.3.0. After subtracting network and broadcast, 1022 hosts are usable.

questions.question: What is CIDR notation (e.g., 192.168.1.0/24)?
options: A way to specify the port number for a service
An IP address with a prefix length indicating how many bits are the network portion
questions.explanation: CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) expresses an IP address and its network mask as address/prefix-length. /24 means the first 24 bits are the network (255.255.255.0), leaving 8 bits for hosts (254 usable). This replaced rigid classful addressing and enables efficient route aggregation.
